questions.question: 1. Which distance metric is also known as the “straight-line” or “as-the-crow-flies” distance?
options: a) Manhattan distance
b) Euclidean distance
c) Cosine distance
d) Chebyshev distance

questions.question: 2. What is the range of values for cosine similarity (not distance)?
options: a) 0 to 1
b) -1 to 1
c) 0 to ∞
d) -∞ to ∞

questions.question: 3. When p = 1 in the Minkowski distance formula, it becomes:
options: a) Euclidean distance
b) Manhattan distance
c) Chebyshev distance
d) Cosine distance

questions.question: 4. Why does Euclidean distance often give poor results when features are on very different scales (e.g., age in years vs income in rupees)?
options: a) It ignores magnitude completely
b) Large-scale features dominate due to squaring
c) It becomes non-metric
d) It only works in 2D space

questions.question: 5. In which scenario is Manhattan distance usually more robust than Euclidean distance?
options: a) When clusters are perfectly spherical
b) When the data contains outliers in individual dimensions
c) When all features are already normalized to unit length
d) When working with text embeddings

questions.question: 6. You compute cosine similarity between two vectors and get ≈ 0.998. What does this tell you?
options: a) The vectors are very far apart in absolute distance
b) The vectors point in almost exactly the same direction
c) The Euclidean distance between them is very small
d) The vectors are almost orthogonal

questions.question: 7. Which of the following is not a valid property of Minkowski distance for p ≥ 1?
options: a) It satisfies the triangle inequality
b) It is zero if and only if the two points are identical
c) It is symmetric
d) It always ignores vector magnitude like cosine does

questions.question: 8. Consider two points A = (1, 100) and B = (2, 200). Their Euclidean distance is ≈ 100.04. Their cosine similarity is very close to 1. Which statement best explains this large difference in behavior?
options: a) Euclidean is scale-invariant, cosine is not
b) Cosine is direction-based and ignores scaling, Euclidean is magnitude-sensitive
c) Manhattan would give exactly the same result as cosine here
d) This only happens when p > 2 in Minkowski

questions.question: 9. In very high-dimensional space (hundreds or thousands of dimensions), which phenomenon makes Euclidean distance less reliable compared to cosine similarity for tasks like text clustering?
options: a) The blessing of dimensionality
b) Distance concentration (most points become almost equidistant)
c) Loss of triangle inequality
d) Increased sensitivity to p in Minkowski

questions.question: 10. You are clustering user movie rating profiles (1–5 stars) where some users rate 10 movies and others rate 200. Many ratings are missing (treated as 0 or skipped). Which combination of representation + distance metric is most commonly recommended in literature and practice?
options: a) Raw ratings + Euclidean distance
b) Mean-centered ratings + Manhattan distance
c) tf-idf-like normalization or row normalization + Cosine similarity
d) Binary (watched / not watched) + Chebyshev distance
